rights.basis: The U-M item labels the materials public domain and links the No Copyright–United States rights statement.

access.accessMethod: Use the public scan of the separately issued Chaucer Society version for page comparison, or request the EETS issue from a holding library.
access.entityLabel: Axel Erdmann, Lydgate's Siege of Thebes, Part I: The Text (EETS e.s. 108, 1911)
notes: The cached public file is not silently substituted for the exact EETS title-page issue, although the text setting and printed pagination agree.

bibliographic.citation: John Lydgate, Lydgate's Siege of Thebes, edited from all the known manuscripts and the two oldest editions, with introduction, notes, and a glossary, by Axel Erdmann, Part I: The Text, EETS Extra Series 108 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society; Oxford University Press, 1911).

bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Extra Series 108
bibliographic.title: Lydgate's Siege of Thebes. Part I.—The Text

entities.description: The immediate source edition represented by U-M: an Arundel 119 base text with marginal summaries and variants from twenty other manuscripts and the two oldest printed editions.
entities.label: Axel Erdmann, Lydgate's Siege of Thebes, Part I: The Text (EETS e.s. 108, 1911)
notes: The exact EETS issue was identified from the encoded title page. The cached Chaucer Society issue preserves the same text pagination but has different issue matter.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1911, more than 95 years before the current year; this status does not determine image-provider terms for a particular scan.
rights.component: 1911 edition text, typography, and apparatus

bibliographic.citation: John Lydgate, Lydgate's Siege of Thebes, ed. Axel Erdmann, Chaucer Society, Second Series 46 (London, 1911, for the issue of 1906).
bibliographic.title: Lydgate's Siege of Thebes. Part I.—The Text

entities.description: The Chaucer Society issue of Erdmann's Part I text represented by the cached Warburg PDF. Its title page identifies 1911 as the date and calls it the issue for 1906.
entities.label: Axel Erdmann, Lydgate's Siege of Thebes, Chaucer Society issue (1911 for 1906)

rights.basis: Published in 1911; the historical issue content is in the U.S. public domain.
rights.component: 1911 Chaucer Society issue text and issue matter

access.accessMethod: Open web edition
access.entityLabel: METS digital edition of The Siege of Thebes

entities.description: A current open scholarly edition with introduction, witness census, source study, and a text freshly checked against Arundel 119. It is a later comparison edition, not an ancestor of CME00051.
entities.label: METS digital edition of The Siege of Thebes

access.accessMethod: British Library digitisation service; request the complete physical manuscript and separately identify Siege ff.1r-79r.
access.entityLabel: British Library Arundel MS 119
notes: No public digital content is linked from the current item record. A complete-codex request should name covers, boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, all 80 leaves, and edges when offered, while mapping the work to ff.1r-79r.

entities.description: Erdmann's base manuscript, an early and complete copy of the Siege written about 1430 and occupying the codex alone. The text ends on f.79r; the volume has 80 vellum leaves.

holding.notes: Erdmann siglum Ar; formerly British Museum, Bibliotheca Arundeliana 119.

entities.label: British Library Arundel MS 119

notes: The sole work in the codex; written for William de la Pole according to current scholarship.

access.accessMethod: CUL Cultural Heritage Imaging Laboratory online image order
access.entityLabel: Cambridge University Library MS Add. 6864
notes: Request the complete 75-folio physical manuscript and binding matter, not only the two already published MSI leaves.

entities.description: A 75-folio paper copy of the Siege, dated by its paper stock to the 1450s-1460s. The work occupies the manuscript; the public digital object exposes only MSI images of ff.5r and 8r.

holding.notes: Erdmann siglum G; the historical Gurney manuscript is now CUL Add. 6864.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Add. 6864

access.accessMethod: Bodleian Imaging Services image order
access.entityLabel: Bodleian Library MS Bodley 776
notes: The public one-leaf slide object does not satisfy a complete physical-manuscript request.

entities.description: An early parchment copy of The Siege of Thebes, dated c. 1430-1440. Digital Bodleian currently publishes a single selected slide image of f.1r, not a whole-codex surrogate.
entities.label: Bodleian Library MS Bodley 776

entities.description: A fifteenth-century parchment composite containing Generydes, Lydgate's Troy Book, and an acephalous Siege of Thebes on ff.191r-211v.
entities.label: Trinity College Cambridge MS O.5.2

notes: The provider sequence includes all three works and surrounding physical matter.

access.accessMethod: No present holding repository is established; trace provenance before seeking reproduction.
access.entityLabel: Former Prince Frederick Duleep Singh manuscript, Old Buckenham Hall

entities.description: The manuscript used under Erdmann's siglum Du. Current scholarship lists the former Duleep Singh/Old Buckenham witness as untraced; a present repository and shelfmark have not been established.
entities.label: Former Prince Frederick Duleep Singh manuscript, Old Buckenham Hall

access.accessMethod: Bodleian Imaging Services order; request the whole physical codex.
access.entityLabel: Bodleian Library MS Laud Misc. 557

entities.description: A second-half fifteenth-century manuscript containing The Siege of Thebes as its principal or sole text; no public whole-codex surrogate was found.
entities.label: Bodleian Library MS Laud Misc. 557

access.accessMethod: Submit Lambeth Palace Library's digital photography form, specifying MS 742 and requesting the whole physical manuscript plus a separate work locus.
access.entityLabel: Lambeth Palace Library MS 742
access.format: High-quality TIFF/JPEG or research PDF depending order
notes: The current form lists high-quality images at £14.40 each including VAT (£12 excluding VAT) and standard microfilm-derived PDFs at £8.40 for the first image and £1.20 each thereafter, with a 40-image order limit. Whole-object feasibility and total price require a quote; publication requires a separate permission application.

entities.description: A fifteenth-century manuscript of The Siege of Thebes used by Erdmann under siglum Lb. No public whole-manuscript digital object was verified; research and reproduction images are orderable from Lambeth Palace Library.
entities.label: Lambeth Palace Library MS 742

entities.description: A complete parchment copy of the Siege on ff.1r-61v, copied by Stephen Doddesham and dated 1425-1450. Yale identifies the manuscript as completely digitized.

holding.notes: Erdmann siglum M; the former Mostyn manuscript is now Beinecke 661.

entities.label: Yale, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, MS 661

access.accessMethod: Bodleian Imaging Services order; obtain an exact work locus from Special Collections before ordering the complete codex.
access.entityLabel: Bodleian Library MS Digby 230

entities.description: A third-quarter fifteenth-century composite containing The Siege of Thebes and Lydgate's Troy Book; no public digital facsimile was found.
entities.label: Bodleian Library MS Digby 230

access.accessMethod: British Library digitisation service; request the complete composite including account wrappers and work ff.1r-before f.30.
access.entityLabel: British Library Add MS 18632

entities.description: A fifteenth-century vellum composite in which The Story of Thebes begins on f.1 and Hoccleve's Regement of Princes begins on f.30; older household-account leaves survive as wrappers.
entities.label: British Library Add MS 18632

notes: The Siege begins f.1; Hoccleve begins f.30, so the work locus ends before f.30 but was not narrowed further here.

access.accessMethod: British Library digitisation service; request the complete 425-folio composite and map the Siege to ff.359v-425v.
access.entityLabel: British Library Add MS 5140
notes: The current catalog retains a legacy digital-images link but explicitly marks those images unavailable after the British Library cyberattack.

entities.description: A c. 1485-1500 paper-and-parchment Canterbury Tales manuscript followed by an imperfect Siege of Thebes on ff.359v-425v.
entities.label: British Library Add MS 5140

access.accessMethod: British Library digitisation service; request the complete composite and map the Siege to ff.292v-341r.
access.entityLabel: British Library Egerton MS 2864
notes: Legacy digital images are currently unavailable; the physical witness also lacks three leaves within the work.

entities.description: A late-fifteenth-century Canterbury Tales composite with an imperfect Siege on ff.292v-341r, missing leaves after ff.335 and 340.
entities.label: British Library Egerton MS 2864

notes: Missing ll.3935-4117 after f.335 and ll.4582-4678 after f.340 according to the current catalog.

access.accessMethod: Bodleian Imaging Services order; request the whole physical codex and separately map Siege ff.5r-78r.
access.entityLabel: Bodleian Library MS Rawl. C. 48

entities.description: A late-fifteenth-century composite codex whose Siege occupies ff.5r-78r. Digital Bodleian publishes one selected slide image of the opening f.5r, not a whole-codex surrogate.
entities.label: Bodleian Library MS Rawl. C. 48

access.accessMethod: Contact Longleat Collections through its access page; ask whether the research-only manuscript microfilm service covers MS 257 and whether a complete digital surrogate can be supplied.
access.entityLabel: Longleat House MS 257
notes: The public page says PDF copies of microfilms for certain manuscripts may be supplied for research only. Coverage of MS 257 and physical-object completeness require confirmation; the Siege occupies ff.1r-48v, and a whole-object request means all extant leaves and binding matter rather than the 28 lost positions in the 212-position collation.

entities.description: A vellum composite whose collation accounts for 212 leaf positions, including 28 lost leaves; The Siege of Thebes occupies ff.1r-48v and is followed by other Middle English works. No authorized public complete facsimile was verified.
entities.label: Longleat House MS 257

physicalDescription.extent: 212 collational leaf positions, including 28 lost leaves; Siege ff.1r-48v

access.accessMethod: British Library digitisation service; request the whole 288-folio composite and map the Siege to ff.17r-83r.
access.entityLabel: British Library Add MS 29729
notes: Legacy digital images are currently unavailable.

entities.description: A paper manuscript copied chiefly by John Stow in 1558; The Siege of Thebes occupies ff.17r-83r within a larger collection of Lydgate works.
entities.label: British Library Add MS 29729

access.accessMethod: CUL Cultural Heritage Imaging Laboratory order; ask Special Collections to confirm collation and work locus before ordering the whole object.
access.entityLabel: Cambridge University Library MS Add. 3137
notes: No public item-level whole-codex facsimile was verified. The 48-versus-61-leaf description conflict should be raised in the enquiry.

entities.description: A parchment copy of the Siege associated with the scribe Stephen Doddesham. Published descriptions disagree on whether the relevant surviving extent should be summarized as 48 or 61 leaves; no public whole-codex surrogate was found.
entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Add. 3137

notes: Erdmann-Ekwall reports 48 leaves; modern scholarship reports 61 parchment leaves plus later matter. The discrepancy remains unresolved.

entities.description: A fifteenth-century parchment composite containing Mandeville's Travels, the Siege on ff.89r-169r, hymns, and surrounding matter.
entities.label: Trinity College Cambridge MS R.4.20

access.accessMethod: Contact the Pepys Librarian for an item record, whole-manuscript photography, exact Siege locus, and reuse terms.
access.entityLabel: Magdalene College, Pepys Library, MS 2011
notes: The Pepys Library is closed to visitors for refurbishment until 2027, but its official page continues to invite advance researcher and photographic-service enquiries.

entities.description: A manuscript copy of The Siege of Thebes collated by Erdmann under siglum P. Its exact work locus and whole-object physical extent have not yet been reverified from an item-level modern catalog.

holding.notes: Erdmann siglum P; held in the Pepys Library.

entities.label: Magdalene College, Pepys Library, MS 2011

access.accessMethod: Bodleian Imaging Services order; request the whole codex and separately identify the Siege beginning f.227r.
access.entityLabel: Bodleian Library MS Laud Misc. 416

entities.description: A fifteenth-century paper composite, dated in part to 1459, in which The Siege of Thebes begins on f.227r.
entities.label: Bodleian Library MS Laud Misc. 416

physicalDescription.extent: At least 289 folios; Siege begins f.227r

access.accessMethod: British Library digitisation service; request the complete 213-folio composite and map the Siege to ff.147v-162r.
access.entityLabel: British Library Royal MS 18 D II
notes: Legacy digital images are currently unavailable; current consultation conditions should be confirmed with the Library.

dateStatements.label: British Library dating for the relevant production phase

entities.description: A mid-fifteenth-century parchment composite whose Siege occupies ff.147v-162r, within a richly varied collection of Middle English literature.
entities.label: British Library Royal MS 18 D II

access.accessMethod: British Library digitisation service; request the whole composite and map the imperfect Siege to ff.3r-51v.
access.entityLabel: British Library Cotton Appendix XXVII

entities.description: A second-half fifteenth-century composite whose imperfect Siege occupies ff.3r-51v, after The Fifteen Joys of Our Lady on ff.1r-2v.
entities.label: British Library Cotton Appendix XXVII

access.accessMethod: Contact St John's College Library for item-level cataloging, exact work locus, digitization status, and whole-manuscript reproduction options.
access.entityLabel: St John's College Cambridge MS 266
notes: No exact public facsimile or item-level access record was verified in this review. The official page says Special Collections can supply formats ranging from private research copies to high-resolution publication images and handles reproduction permission separately.

entities.description: The manuscript identified by current scholarship as Wynkyn de Worde's copy-text for the c. 1497 print. It was not one of Erdmann's twenty-one manuscript sigla and is collateral to his print witness E1.
entities.label: St John's College Cambridge MS 266

access.accessMethod: EEBO/ProQuest page images of the imperfect British Library copy
access.entityLabel: Wynkyn de Worde, The Story and Destruction of Thebes (c. 1497)
access.format: EEBO page images; four leaves lacking
notes: No complete public facsimile or authorized direct download was found. The TCP CC0 grant applies to the keyboarded text and markup, not these page images.

rights.basis: The early printed work predates modern copyright; EEBO access and image rights remain separate and subscription-mediated.
rights.component: Underlying c. 1497 printed text and typography, excluding modern EEBO photographs and TCP markup

bibliographic.citation: John Lydgate, Prologus: Here begynneth the prologue of the storye of Thebes ([Westminster]: Wynkyn de Worde, [1497?]); STC 17031, ESTC S120573.
bibliographic.title: Prologus Here begynneth the prologue of the storye of Thebes

entities.description: The first known printed edition, produced by Wynkyn de Worde at Westminster about 1497 and collated by Erdmann as E1. The EEBO/TCP source copy at the British Library is imperfect, lacking four leaves.
entities.label: Wynkyn de Worde, The Story and Destruction of Thebes (c. 1497)

rights.basis: The early printed work predates modern copyright; EEBO access and image rights remain separate and subscription-mediated.
rights.component: Underlying c. 1497 printed text and typography, excluding modern EEBO photographs and TCP markup

bibliographic.citation: The woorkes of Geffrey Chaucer, newly printed, with divers additions, edited by William Thynne with additions by John Stow (London: John Kingston for John Wight, 1561), including Lydgate's Siege and Destruction of Thebes; STC 5076, ESTC S107207.
bibliographic.title: The woorkes of Geffrey Chaucer, newly printed, with divers additions

entities.description: The 1561 Chaucer collected edition printed by John Kingston for John Wight, incorporating additions by John Stow and including the Siege. Erdmann collated it as E2. Modern analysis finds dependence on de Worde for about the first 400 lines and a different manuscript branch thereafter.
entities.label: John Stow's Siege of Thebes in The Workes of Geffrey Chaucer (1561)

rights.basis: The early printed volume predates modern copyright.
rights.component: Underlying 1561 printed text and typography, excluding modern photography

reviewNotes: Erdmann's printed key, not intuitive siglum expansion, controls the twenty-one manuscript and two early-print source identities.

The cached 1911 volume has a Chaucer Society title page ('1911, for the Issue of 1906') while the U-M title page identifies the EETS/Oxford University Press issue. They are distinct issues linked as versions, not collapsed into one object.
Current scholarship reports thirty-one manuscripts. Witnesses identified after Erdmann's work belong to the broader tradition but are not added to his historical collation chain.
The current schema's scholarly_edition entity type is used provisionally for the c.1497 and 1561 printed editions; it records a printed textual layer and does not imply modern critical editing.

The Yale and Trinity whole-object caches contain every canvas exposed by their provider manifests. Their work_portion fields point to the narrower Siege loci, and their notes state which physical exterior views the providers do not expose.
Underlying medieval and early-print texts, modern editorial content, digital markup, manuscript photography, catalog metadata, and provider contracts receive separate rights treatment.

lineage.summary: CME00051 is the General Corpus XML representation of John Lydgate's Middle English Siege of Thebes. The repository artifact copies the University of Michigan encoding of Axel Erdmann's 1911 EETS text. Erdmann based the text on British Library Arundel MS 119 and collated twenty additional manuscripts plus the c. 1497 Wynkyn de Worde and 1561 John Stow prints. This graph keeps the work, repository artifact, digital encodings, bibliographically distinct 1911 issues, companion 1930 apparatus, twenty-one collated manuscripts, early prints, later modern edition, facsimiles, access routes, and rights layers separate. Where a public whole-manuscript surrogate is available, the Git-ignored cache retains the complete provider sequence and maps the work's folios and canvases inside it; selected-image objects remain explicitly partial.

catalogSummary.author: John Lydgate
catalogSummary.region: England; Thebes and the Canterbury return route as narrative settings
catalogSummary.title: The Siege of Thebes

catalogingSubject.label: The Siege of Thebes
catalogingSubject.scope_note: John Lydgate's Middle English verse romance The Siege of Thebes, including its 176-line return-pilgrimage prologue and three numbered parts. The intellectual work is distinct from its manuscript witnesses and early prints, Axel Erdmann's 1911 critical text and the posthumous 1930 apparatus volume completed by Eilert Ekwall, the University of Michigan encoding, the repository XML, and all photographic or digital reproductions.

contentParts.label: Prologue
contentParts.summary: Lydgate dates and stages the homeward pilgrimage, meets Chaucer's company, describes himself and Canterbury, and accepts the Host's command to tell a tale.

contentParts.label: Part I

locators.value: pp. 10-44, lines 177-1046; numbered line 389 absent from the base-text stream

contentParts.summary: The tale begins beside the pilgrims and recounts the foundation of Thebes, its early rulers, and the history of Oedipus through the transition to his sons.

contentParts.label: Part II

locators.value: pp. 45-105, lines 1047-2552; numbered lines 1062 and 1747 absent from the base-text stream

contentParts.summary: The return-journey frame resumes near Boughton; the alternating kingship of Eteocles and Polynices fails, Tydeus carries an embassy and survives ambush, and preparations for war gather force.

contentParts.label: Part III
contentParts.summary: The Seven advance against Thebes, the war destroys its heroes and the rival brothers, the city falls, and a prayer for charity, peace, concord, and unity closes the poem.

assertion.notes: The fictional return journey is astronomically dated to 27 April 1421, while Henry V's death on 31 August 1422 supplies the usual terminus ante quem.

notes: This dates the earliest presently identified manuscript witness, not composition itself.

dateStatements.display: c. 1497, printed by Wynkyn de Worde

extent.description: The scholarly lineation runs 1-4716 across a 176-line prologue and three parts (177-1046, 1047-2552, and 2553-4716). The XML main stream contains 4,713 L elements because Erdmann's Arundel-based text skips numbered lines 389, 1062, and 1747; four variant lines for 1017-1020 are nested inside a footnote, so the raw file-wide count is 4,717 L. The file also contains 5 LG, 194 PB, 1,338 MILESTONE, 4,120 NOTE, and 5 P elements. These are representation metrics rather than universal manuscript extents.

formStatements.description: Long narrative verse, predominantly in decasyllabic rhyming couplets, with a 176-line prologue and three numbered parts.

genres.label: Classical romance
notes: The poem recasts legendary Theban history as a medieval romance of rulers, counsel, war, Fortune, and civic destruction.

genres.label: Canterbury Tales continuation
notes: Lydgate presents the work as the first tale told on the pilgrims' return journey; this framing does not make it a work by Chaucer.

genres.label: Mirror for princes
notes: Political counsel is integrated into romance rather than forming a freestanding conduct tract.

Erdmann's historical edition key and current repositories are kept in the lineage manifest. Current scholarship reports thirty-one manuscripts, but only the twenty-one manuscripts and two early prints actually collated by Erdmann are upstream of the 1911 edition.

The XML includes marginal summaries, textual variants, and manuscript-leaf milestones inherited from Erdmann. They document the edition and encoding; they are not independent prose sections of Lydgate's poem.

The work's underlying medieval text is distinct from image licenses on manuscript photography. Cached whole-manuscript surrogates remain Git-ignored and their work folio/canvas portions are mapped separately.

next_steps: Review current source studies and compare the poem passage by passage with surviving French prose redactions without identifying a surviving copy as Lydgate's exemplar absent provenance evidence.
openQuestions.question: Which exact version or exemplar of the French Roman de Edipus did Lydgate use, and how did it interact with the Hystoire de Thebes and Latin sources?

next_steps: Review biographical, patronage, astronomical, and linguistic scholarship on the 1421-1422 composition window.
openQuestions.question: Can the place and circumstances of composition be localized more narrowly than England without transferring an early witness's dialect or origin to Lydgate's authorial text?

next_steps: Inspect the corresponding Arundel leaves and Erdmann-Ekwall 1930 notes before assigning reconstructed text to any skipped number.
openQuestions.question: Do Erdmann's skipped base-text numbers 389, 1062, and 1747 represent Arundel omissions, editorially reserved readings, or another explicit lineation convention in the 1930 apparatus?

notes: No narrower composition place is asserted, and the localization of individual scribes is not transferred to the work.

scope.description: The legendary Theban history narrated inside the pilgrimage frame
The pilgrims' fictional homeward journey after Chaucer's Canterbury Tales

notes: No single physical exemplar has been established; adaptation is used instead of asserting a direct translation from one surviving source copy.

scope.description: Middle English retelling of the Theban story from French prose, Latin mythographic, classical, biblical, and Chaucerian materials
The 1911 critical text, including its marginal glosses and textual apparatus
Base text from British Library Arundel MS 119
Variants from twenty additional manuscripts and the two oldest printed editions

subjects.label: Thebes and the Seven against Thebes
Fratricide, civil war, and civic destruction

summaries.value: On the fictional homeward journey after Chaucer's Canterbury pilgrimage, Lydgate joins the company and is required by the Host to tell the first return tale. He recounts the foundation and destruction of Thebes: the history of Oedipus, the divided rule and fatal quarrel of Eteocles and Polynices, the embassy and exploits of Tydeus, the Seven against Thebes, fratricidal war, and the city's ruin. The poem repeatedly turns legendary history into counsel about prudent rule, truthful promises, peace, civic unity, and the destructiveness of private ambition.

Current scholarship reports thirty-one manuscripts, while Erdmann's 1911 edition used Arundel MS 119 as its base and collated twenty additional manuscripts plus the c. 1497 de Worde and 1561 Stow prints. Those historical witnesses and editorial layers are recorded in the paired lineage manifest; later discoveries do not retroactively become Erdmann's sources.

notes: The XML ends with the rubric 'Here endeth the destruction of Thebes'; Bodley 776 and Beinecke 661 also preserve this discovery form.

titles.value: Whan brighte Phebus passed was the Ram
Here endeth the destruction of Thebes

work.summary: On the fictional homeward journey after Chaucer's Canterbury pilgrimage, Lydgate joins the company and is required by the Host to tell the first return tale. He recounts the foundation and destruction of Thebes: the history of Oedipus, the divided rule and fatal quarrel of Eteocles and Polynices, the embassy and exploits of Tydeus, the Seven against Thebes, fratricidal war, and the city's ruin. The poem repeatedly turns legendary history into counsel about prudent rule, truthful promises, peace, civic unity, and the destructiveness of private ambition.
